description: CFO Jean Jones of M57.Biz received spoofed emails apparently from president Alison requesting a spreadsheet with confidential employee data. The attacker manipulated the Reply-To to exfiltrate the data to tuckergorge@gmail.com. Jean acted in good faith but the attack resulted in exfiltration of confidential documents.
tier_note: Soluciones ampliamente publicadas. No usar para scoring; sí para desarrollo.

artifacts.content: Email #1: From: alison@m57.biz | To: jean@m57.biz | Date: 2008-07-19 16:39:57 PDT | Subject: background checks | Return-Path: simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com [SPOOFED] | Reply-To: alison@m57.biz (legitimate) | Note: 'please do not include the text of this email in your reply'
forensic_anomalies: Actual Return-Path (simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com) does not match the From address (alison@m57.biz)
The note 'do not include the text' is highly suspicious as an attempt to destroy evidence

artifacts.content: Email #2: From: alison@m57.biz | To: jean@m57.biz | Date: 2008-07-19 18:22:45 PDT | Subject: Please send me the information now | Return-Path: simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com [SPOOFED] | Reply-To: tuckergorge@gmail.com [MALICIOUS - CHANGED from email #1]
forensic_anomalies: Reply-To changed from alison@m57.biz to tuckergorge@gmail.com between consecutive emails
The same spoofed Return-Path indicates a coordinated phishing campaign
REPLY-TO CHANGE between consecutive emails is the definitive signature of the spoofing attack

artifacts.content: File: m57biz.xls | Location: Jean's Desktop (C:/Documents and Settings/Jean/Desktop/) | Creation date: 07/20/2008 1:28:03 AM GMT | Encrypted: No | Content: Names, positions, salaries ($1,009,000 total) and SSNs of 8 employees | Initial sector: 5,160,295
forensic_anomalies: File with SSNs and salaries stored unencrypted on Desktop
The file was created minutes before being sent to the malicious Reply-To

artifacts.content: Alison's reply to Jean: 'What's a sure thing?' (16:50:20 PDT) | Exfiltration email: Jean → tuckergorge@gmail.com with attachment M57biz.xls (18:28:45 PDT) | Attacker's email #3: 'Thanks!' (22:03:40 PDT)
forensic_anomalies: Alison's confusion proves she did not send the original email
The 6-hour window between first email and attacker's confirmation shows a compressed attack window

artifacts.content: Deleted system binaries: ipconfig.exe, net.exe | AIM6 installed with suspicious toolbar bundles | Cached HTML files: rcstatus.htm, InstallStatus[1].htm (fake Microsoft prompts) | Remote Assistance artifacts in Windows Help directories
forensic_anomalies: Deletion of system binaries is an indicator of anti-forensics or prior compromise
Presence of HTML with fake Microsoft prompts suggests additional social engineering techniques
